title: Combating Cartels on Social Media Act of 2025
short_title: Combating Cartels on Social Media Act of 2025
plain_summary: This law tells three government departments to work together to stop foreign criminal groups from using social media to find and hire people in America for illegal jobs. These criminal groups use apps and websites to recruit people for things like smuggling drugs or people across the border. The government agencies must study how this recruiting happens and then make a plan to stop it. They have to make sure their plan does not hurt people's rights or privacy while they work to keep Americans safe from being recruited by these dangerous groups.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Combating Cartels on Social Media Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2  Definitions
sections.text: In this law, the following terms mean these things. Appropriate congressional committees means the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs, the Committee on the Judiciary, and the Committee on Foreign Relations of the Senate. It also means the Committee on Homeland Security, the Committee on the Judiciary, and the Committee on Foreign Affairs of the House of Representatives. Covered operator means the company or person who operates, develops, or publishes a covered service. Covered service means a social media platform. It also means a mobile or desktop service that lets people send direct messages or group messages, but not basic text messaging or email services. The Secretary of Homeland Security must decide that criminal organizations from other countries are using or have used these services for the illegal activities described in section three. Covered service also means a digital platform or app that lets multiple people talk to each other in real time, including video games with multiple players and virtual reality platforms or apps. Again, the Secretary of Homeland Security must decide that criminal organizations from other countries are using or have used these services for the illegal activities described in section three. Criminal enterprise has the same meaning as continuing criminal enterprise in section four hundred eight of the Controlled Substances Act. Illicit activities means criminal activities that happen across national borders. These include violating section four hundred one of the Controlled Substances Act, which covers illegal drug activities. They include narcotics trafficking as defined in section eight hundred eight of the Foreign Narcotics Kingpin Designation Act. They include weapons trafficking. They include migrant smuggling, which is violating section two hundred seventy four of the Immigration and Nationality Act. They include human trafficking, which is violating sections fifteen ninety, fifteen ninety one, or fifteen ninety two of title eighteen of the United States Code, or engaging in severe forms of trafficking in persons as defined in section one hundred three of the Victims of Trafficking and Violence Protection Act of two thousand. They include cyber crime, which is violating section ten thirty of title eighteen of the United States Code. They include violating any law about intellectual property enforcement as defined in section three hundred two of the Prioritizing Resources and Organization for Intellectual Property Act of two thousand eight. They include bulk cash smuggling of currency, which is violating section fifty three thirty two of title thirty one of the United States Code. They also include laundering money from any of these criminal activities. Transnational criminal organization means a group, network, and associated individuals that operate across countries to get power, influence, or money, wholly or partly through illegal means. They advance their activities through a pattern of crime, corruption, or violence. They protect their illegal activities through an organizational structure that crosses countries and by exploiting public corruption or systems for logistics, finance, or communication that cross countries.

sections.title: Section 3  Assessment of Illicit Usage
sections.text: No later than one hundred eighty days after this law is passed, the Secretary of Homeland Security, the Attorney General, and the Secretary of State must submit a joint assessment to the appropriate congressional committees. This assessment must describe how transnational criminal organizations or criminal enterprises working for them use covered services to recruit people, including people in the United States, to engage in or support illegal activities happening in the United States, Mexico, or near an international border of the United States. The assessment must describe how these organizations use covered services to engage in illegal activities or conduct that supports illegal activities. This includes smuggling or trafficking drugs, other controlled substances, their ingredients, or other items that are illegal under United States, Mexican, or other relevant laws, including firearms. It includes human smuggling or trafficking, especially the exploitation of children. It includes transporting large amounts of cash or monetary instruments to help with smuggling or trafficking. The assessment must also describe existing efforts by the Secretary of Homeland Security, the Attorney General, the Secretary of State, and relevant government and law enforcement agencies to counter, monitor, or otherwise respond to the use of covered services described above.

sections.title: Section 4  Strategy to Combat Cartel Recruitment on Social Media and Online Platforms
sections.text: No later than one year after this law is passed, the Secretary of Homeland Security, the Attorney General, and the Secretary of State must submit a joint strategy to the appropriate congressional committees. This strategy will be called the National Strategy to Combat Illicit Recruitment Activity by Transnational Criminal Organizations on Social Media and Online Platforms. The strategy must combat the use of covered services by transnational criminal organizations or criminal enterprises working for them to recruit people in the United States to engage in or support unlawful activities happening in the United States, Mexico, or near an international border of the United States. The strategy must include at least these elements. It must propose ways to improve cooperation between the Secretary of Homeland Security, the Attorney General, the Secretary of State, and relevant law enforcement agencies. It must recommend ways to create a process for voluntary reporting of information about recruitment efforts by transnational criminal organizations or criminal enterprises working for them in the United States involving covered services. It must propose ways to improve coordination within the government about these matters, including between the Department of Homeland Security, the Department of Justice, the Department of State, and state, tribal, and local governments. It must propose ways to improve coordination within the Department of Homeland Security, the Department of Justice, and the Department of State and between the parts of those departments about these matters. It must include activities to help increase intelligence analysis for law enforcement purposes of efforts by transnational criminal organizations or criminal enterprises working for them to use covered services for recruitment to engage in or support illegal activities. It must include activities to build international partnerships and improve collaboration with foreign governments and, when applicable, multilateral institutions about these matters. It must include activities to specifically increase engagement and outreach with young people in border communities, including information about recruitment tactics of transnational criminal organizations or criminal enterprises working for them and the consequences of participating in illegal activities. It must include a detailed description of measures used to ensure that law enforcement and intelligence activities focus on the recruitment activities of transnational criminal organizations or criminal enterprises working for them, rather than on individuals these organizations or enterprises try to recruit or successfully recruit. It must also ensure protection of privacy rights, civil rights, and civil liberties when carrying out these activities, with special focus on protections for minors and constitutionally protected activities. The strategy cannot include recommendations for new laws or elements that depend on the passage of laws that have not been enacted when the strategy is submitted. When creating and implementing the strategy, the Secretary of Homeland Security, the Attorney General, and the Secretary of State must consult and engage with specific officials from their departments and other agencies. They must consult with the Secretary of Health and Human Services and the Secretary of Education. The Secretary of Homeland Security or a designee from the Office of Public Engagement must select representatives of border communities to consult with, including representatives of state, tribal, and local governments, school districts, and local law enforcement. They must also consult with nongovernmental experts in civil rights and civil liberties, online privacy, humanitarian assistance for migrants, and youth outreach and rehabilitation. No later than ninety days after the strategy is submitted to the appropriate congressional committees, the Secretary of Homeland Security, the Attorney General, and the Secretary of State must start implementing the strategy. No later than one hundred eighty days after the strategy is implemented, and then every six months for five years, these officials must submit a joint report to the appropriate congressional committees. This report must describe their efforts to implement the strategy and the progress of those efforts. The report must describe the recommendations and how they are being implemented regarding voluntary reporting processes. It must describe how different agencies are working together on matters covered by the strategy, including collaboration between federal agencies, state, local, and tribal agencies, foreign governments, and multilateral institutions when applicable. It must describe the threat landscape, including new developments related to recruitment efforts by transnational criminal organizations or criminal enterprises working for them, and their use of new or emerging covered services and recruitment methods. Each report must be submitted in unclassified form but may contain a classified annex (secret section). No later than two years after the strategy is implemented, the Office for Civil Rights and Civil Liberties and the Privacy Office of the Department of Homeland Security must submit a joint report to the appropriate congressional committees. They must work with the Assistant Attorney General for the Civil Rights Division and the Chief Privacy and Civil Liberties Officer of the Department of Justice on this report. The report must include a detailed assessment of measures used to ensure protection of civil rights, civil liberties, and privacy rights when carrying out this section. It must include recommendations to improve implementation of the strategy. Before implementing the strategy at the Department of Homeland Security, the Secretary of Homeland Security must issue rules to carry out this section according to section five hundred fifty three of title five of the United States Code.

sections.title: Section 5  Rule of Construction
sections.text: Nothing in this law can be understood to expand the legal law enforcement or regulatory authority of the Department of Homeland Security, the Department of Justice, or the Department of State.

sections.title: Section 6  No Additional Funds
sections.text: No additional money is authorized to be set aside by Congress for the purpose of carrying out this law.
